toc_title: Authentication and authorization in your app
title: Authentication and authorization in your app - Azure Fluid Relay
display_title: Implement authentication and authorization for Azure Fluid Relay apps
description: Overview of how to use authentication and authorization with an Azure Fluid Relay service.
summary: Security is critical to modern web applications. Fluid Framework, as a part of your web application architecture is an important piece of infrastructure to secure. Fluid Framework is a layered architecture, and auth-related concepts are implemented based on the Fluid service it's connecting to. This means that, although there are common authentication themes across all Fluid services, the details and specifics will differ for each service.
classification_reason: Covers how auth is layered and implemented per Fluid service, with Azure Fluid Relay–specific auth details; this is product-specific security configuration guidance.

toc_title: Container management
title: Managing Fluid containers - Azure Fluid Relay
description: Overview of how to manage containers in Azure Fluid Relay service.
summary: A Container is the atomic unit of storage in the Azure Fluid Relay service and represents the data stored from a Fluid session, including operations and snapshots. The Fluid runtime uses the container to rehydrate the state of a Fluid session when a user joins for the first time or rejoins after leaving. When building an application with the Fluid Framework, there are several things you need to account for regarding container creation and management, as summarized in this diagram.
classification_reason: Explains container concepts and lifecycle at a high level; lacks specific configuration parameters, limits, or error-resolution flows.

toc_title: Customer-managed keys for Azure Fluid Relay encryption
title: Customer-managed keys for Azure Fluid Relay encryption - Azure Fluid Relay
display_title: Configure customer-managed keys for Azure Fluid Relay
description: Better understand the data encryption with CMK
summary: You can use your own encryption key to protect the data in your Azure Fluid Relay resource. When you specify a customer-managed key (CMK), that key is used to protect and control access to the key that encrypts your data. CMK offers greater flexibility to manage access controls. You must use one of the following Azure key stores to store your CMK: You must create a new Azure Fluid Relay resource to enable CMK. You cannot change the CMK enablement/disablement on an existing Fluid Relay resource.
classification_reason: Details CMK usage, required Azure key stores, and one-way enablement behavior for new resources; these are product-specific encryption and key management behaviors.

toc_title: Data encryption in Azure Fluid Relay
title: Data encryption in Azure Fluid Relay - Azure Fluid Relay
display_title: Understand Azure Fluid Relay data encryption architecture
description: Better understand the data encryption in Fluid Relay Server
summary: Azure Fluid Relay leverages the encryption-at-rest capability ofAzure Kubernetes Service,Azure Cosmos DBandAzure Blob Storage. The service-to-service communication between Azure Fluid Relay and these resources is TLS encrypted and is enclosed in with the Azure Virtual Network boundary, protected from external interference by Network Security Rules. The diagram below shows at a high level how Azure Fluid Relay is implemented and how it handles data storage.
classification_reason: Describes how Fluid Relay uses AKS, Cosmos DB, and Blob Storage encryption-at-rest and TLS within a VNet with Network Security Rules; this is product-specific security implementation detail beyond generic concepts.

toc_title: How to: Recover Container data
title: Recovering Fluid data - Azure Fluid Relay
display_title: Recover corrupted Azure Fluid Relay container data
description: Learn how to recover container data
summary: In this scenario, we explore data recovery. We consider data to be corrupted when container reaches an invalid state where it can't process further user actions. The outcome of corrupted state is container being unexpectedly closed. Often it's transient state, and upon reopening, the container may behave as expected. In a situation where a container fails to load even after multiple retries, we offer APIs and flows you can use to recover your data, as described below.
classification_reason: Defines corrupted container states, transient vs persistent failures, and provides APIs/flows for data recovery; this is a symptom-to-solution troubleshooting guide specific to Fluid Relay.

toc_title: How to: Validate a User Created a Document
title: How to: Validate a user who created a container - Azure Fluid Relay
display_title: Validate container creator identity in Azure Fluid Relay
description: How to validate that the user who created a container is the same user who is claiming to be accessing the container.
summary: When you create a container in Azure Fluid Relay, the JWT provided by the ITokenProvider for the creation request can only be used once. After creating a container, the client must generate a new JWT that contains the document ID (which is really the container ID) provided by the service at creation time. If an application has an authorization service that manages container access control, it needs to know who created a container with a given ID in order to authorize the generation of a new JWT
classification_reason: Explains one-time JWT usage for container creation and how to verify the creator when issuing new JWTs; this is a product-specific authorization pattern.
